title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: A court ruling reopens a massive AI data center project, raising fresh questions on power, water and local say.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Today we're digging into a court decision that just overturned a township's rejection of a huge AI campus, and what that means for neighbors facing power lines, wells, and roads.
That sounds like it shifts the whole fight from town hall to the courtroom.
Exactly, and it's the lead story because it happened today.
So walk me through what the court actually did.
A township had turned down this $16 billion AI campus project, but a judge stepped in and reopened the door for it to move forward.
What was the township's main reason for saying no in the first place?
They were worried about the scale — hundreds of megawatts of power draw, heavy water use for cooling, and strain on local roads and wells.
That makes sense for any rural or suburban area. How common are these local vetoes lately?
We've seen several townships try to hit pause or set strict rules this year, but courts are starting to get involved more often.
Who are the main players here — the developer, the township, and the state?
It's a big tech-backed project, the township board voted it down on zoning, and now the developer is using the courts to push back.
And the state? Any role yet?
Not directly in this ruling, but Governor Whitmer has been pushing faster approvals statewide, which adds another layer.
Okay, so the decision moves the battle to court. What does that mean for electric bills?
Neighbors are asking who pays for new power lines and upgrades — and whether data centers will cover those costs or if ratepayers get hit.
We've heard about agreements where tech companies promise to pay for infrastructure. Does that apply here?
Those deals are happening in other cases, but this ruling doesn't settle the funding question yet.
Water is another big one. What are residents worried about with their wells?
Massive server farms need a lot of water for cooling, and in areas already watching groundwater, folks fear shortages or contamination risks.
Farmland and open space too — does the project sit on ag land?
Many of these proposals do, and losing that acreage changes the local tax base and character of the community.
Roads sound like an everyday impact. How much extra truck traffic are we talking?
Construction alone brings heavy loads, and once running, there are still maintenance and supply runs that wear on rural roads not built for it.
So what happens next — does the township appeal, or does the project start moving?
The developer can now proceed with permitting while the township decides whether to fight the ruling further.
Any timeline on when construction could actually begin?
Nothing firm yet, but these projects can move fast once legal hurdles clear.
How are other townships watching this?
Closely — places like Monroe County just paused new permits for 18 months today to buy time and study impacts.
That pause includes battery storage too, right?
Yes, because the two often come together and pull similar resources from the grid and water supply.
Back to this case, what about noise or other quality-of-life issues?
A separate lawsuit filed today in West Michigan claims constant noise from an existing data center is already disrupting homes.
Interesting how these stories are piling up on the same day.
It shows the side effects are hitting real people now.
What can residents do if they feel blindsided by a court ruling like this?
Public comment at planning meetings, contacting commissioners, and watching for appeals are the main levers left.
Any chance the state steps in with new rules after this?
The GOP chair floated a ban yesterday on utility deals that shield data centers from rate hikes, so the conversation is definitely heating up.
That would change how billions in grid upgrades get paid for.
Exactly — ratepayers in DTE and Consumers territories would feel it most.
Tech giants also agreed yesterday to help pay for power infrastructure. How does that fit?
It's an effort to keep households from footing the full bill, and Michigan officials secured those commitments.
Sounds like a step in the right direction, but is it enough for this project?
It sets a precedent, though each deal is negotiated separately.
Switching gears a bit, Saline Township dropped its cap on incentives yesterday. What changed there?
They removed the proposed limit on tax breaks, opening the door to bigger incentives for a project that could draw hundreds of megawatts.
Residents 30 minutes away could still feel it through schools and roads.
That's the ripple effect we're seeing across the state.
Another township is battling the state data center law while Whitmer rolls out a new pledge.
Yes, locals want more say on noise, power strain, and water use, but state rules limit their blocking power.
On Monday we had utility allies going after a data center critic.
Groups tied to Consumers Energy are pushing back on a vocal opponent as power costs rise.
Negaunee has a hearing set for August 13 on zoning rules.
Marquette County residents will weigh in on where and how large data centers can locate.
And Manchester granted a tax break after slashing the request by 89 percent.
The project still gets relief, but revenue for schools and services will be lower than first hoped.
That's a lot happening in one week.
It really is.
Let's talk about the meeting coming up tomorrow.
The Charter Township of Oxford Planning Commission meets July 23 to discuss a draft data center ordinance.
No action expected, but they want public input on zoning rules for future projects.
Perfect chance for residents to shape what might come.
Anyone thinking about attending should check the agenda for details.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.
Thanks for listening.

episode_title: Court Revives $16B AI Campus After Township Veto Overturned
